localBiteReport: Reports from the past week out of Santa Rosa Beach and Destin area show solid inshore action on Choctawhatchee Bay. Speckled trout have been showing up in good numbers, and bull redfish are also active — one report from Santa Rosa Beach this week noted a 26-lb bull red landed in the bay. Charter anglers fishing the bay have been finding redfish and flounder alongside trout, with early mornings being the most productive window given summer heat and water temps at 84°F. Wind has been a factor at times, pushing anglers to be selective about which areas of the bay they target, but when conditions cooperate the bite has been strong.

aiSummary: Choctawhatchee Bay is producing solid inshore action this week, with speckled trout, redfish, and flounder all showing up for charter and recreational anglers — including a 26-lb bull red reported out of the bay recently. Today's window is tight: storms are likely after 11 AM with an 83% chance, so plan to be on the water at first light and off by mid-morning. The ESE wind at 15 mph and slightly stained water at 84°F will favor protected bayous and current-driven structure over open-bay spots.

bridge.aiNote: The 331 Bridge spans the narrow neck of Choctawhatchee Bay, creating a current funnel that pushes bait through on both tides. Pilings hold speckled trout, redfish, sheepshead, and black drum year-round, with the lee-side pilings serving as the prime ambush zone on incoming tides. Being open water, this spot is the first to become unfishable when wind and chop build.
bridge.todaysCall: With ESE winds at 15 mph, the 331 Bridge's open exposure will likely mean choppy, uncomfortable conditions — and that's before storms arrive after 11 AM. If you go, hit the lee-side pilings early at first light while the tide is still moving toward low at 11:14 AM; bait will be funneling and predators will be active. Slightly stained water actually helps here — fish tuck tight to structure and are less line-shy. Be realistic about your exit window: pack up well before 11 AM to stay ahead of the storm threat. This spot rewards an early commitment today but punishes anyone who lingers.

alaqua.aiNote: Alaqua Bayou offers well-protected water with a strong year-round redfish population, plus trout and flounder concentrated near the bayou mouth. The oyster bars at the creek mouth are the prime target, and tree cover keeps temperatures a few degrees cooler than the open bay while also providing shelter from wind. The upper creek transitions toward brackish water and holds bass alongside smaller redfish.
alaqua.todaysCall: Alaqua is a smart pick today — the tree cover and protected layout will buffer the ESE 15 mph wind, and you can fish comfortably well past the point where open-bay spots become untenable. Focus on the oyster bars at the mouth during the morning ebb toward low tide at 11:14 AM; moving water will stack bait and draw reds and trout into feeding position. Slightly stained water suits this spot — redfish hunt by feel and lateral line along those oyster edges regardless of clarity. With the storm window opening after 11 AM, plan to push up into the creek interior if weather builds, buying yourself extra time under tree cover. The bay-wide bite report confirms reds and trout are active, and Alaqua's resident redfish population makes it one of the better bets for a quality fish today.

basin.aiNote: Basin Bayou sees less fishing pressure than Alaqua and LaGrange, which tends to mean less-educated fish. Interior grass flats are classic sight-fishing territory for redfish in under two feet of water, and pompano occasionally appear at the mouth on moving tides in late spring and early summer. It is less protected than LaGrange when weather builds across the bay.
basin.todaysCall: Basin Bayou's lower pressure makes it appealing any day, and this morning could be productive on the falling tide toward the 11:14 AM low — work the interior grass flats for reds early while light and wind are still manageable. Slightly stained water will limit sight-fishing effectiveness on the flats, so lean toward moving presentations along grass edges rather than spot-and-stalk. The ESE wind at 15 mph is a consideration here since Basin offers less weather protection than LaGrange; if chop builds inside the bayou, you'll want to reposition or bail earlier than you might expect. With the 83% storm chance kicking in after 11 AM, treat your hard out as 10:30 AM at this location. Get there at first light to maximize your productive window.

lagrange.aiNote: LaGrange Bayou is the top trout producer among the bayous on this side of the bay, with legendary oyster bars at the mouth and interior grass flats that hold redfish year-round. Deep holes inside harbor black drum and sheepshead, adding variety to the target list. It is the most protected water on this side of the bay, making it the default destination when weather builds.
lagrange.todaysCall: LaGrange is the best all-around choice for today given the storm threat — it's the most protected bayou on this side of the bay, meaning you can keep fishing even as conditions deteriorate elsewhere. Work the oyster bars at the mouth hard during the morning ebb toward the 11:14 AM low tide; first-quarter moon provides a moderate tidal push that will keep bait moving and predators active. Slightly stained water actually plays into the strength of this spot — trout and reds are keyed on the oyster structure by current and sound, not just sight. The bay-wide bite report confirms trout and reds are in good shape right now, and LaGrange's consistent production makes it a high-confidence play. If storms push in early, simply move to the deep interior holes for black drum and sheepshead and extend your day safely.

fourmile.aiNote: Four Mile Creek is a tidal creek running between Shipyard Marina and the bay, making it the most convenient option for anglers launching from that ramp. Tidal flow pushes baitfish up the creek on incoming tides, concentrating redfish and trout in the bends and along grass edges. The creek mouth is a prime flounder zone, and the upper section holds bass and smaller redfish where the water transitions toward brackish. It is short and well-protected, making it the safest fishery when weather threatens.
fourmile.todaysCall: Four Mile Creek is purpose-built for a day like today — it's the most sheltered option on the dashboard and the quickest to reach from Shipyard Marina, giving you maximum fishing time and a fast escape route when storms arrive after 11 AM. This morning's falling tide toward the 11:14 AM low will push baitfish toward the creek mouth, so position at the mouth early for flounder and work up into the bends for redfish and trout as the tide drops. ESE wind at 15 mph is essentially a non-issue inside this creek. Slightly stained water is normal for this creek environment and won't hurt the bite — reds and trout are keyed on current-driven bait in the bends. If you're a cautious planner or fishing with kids or beginners today, this is the spot.

hogtown.aiNote: Hogtown Bayou is a signature Santa Rosa Beach guide destination and one of the most consistently productive trout flats on the bay. Summer mornings are the peak window, with trout stacked on grass in two to four feet of water. Spanish mackerel show at the mouth on moving tides, and the bayou sees regular pressure but continues to produce reliably. Factor in the longer run time from the 331 Bridge side of the bay.
hogtown.todaysCall: Hogtown is worth the run if you leave at first light — summer mornings are peak time here, and the bay-wide report confirms trout and redfish are active, which aligns perfectly with what this flat produces best. Arrive early and work the grass in two to four feet of water while the tide is still on the fall toward the 11:14 AM low, as moving water keeps trout activated and positioned on the flat. Slightly stained water reduces sight-fishing precision, so rely on topwaters or soft plastics worked methodically across the flat rather than targeting specific fish visually. The storm window after 11 AM is your hard limit — Hogtown's distance from the marina means you need to budget return run time into your departure decision. Be on your way back no later than 10:15 to 10:30 AM to stay ahead of the 83% storm probability.

rocky.aiNote: Rocky Bayou sits inside Fred Gannon Rocky Bayou State Park near Niceville, offering a protected, scenic, and significantly less pressured alternative to the bayous closer to the 331 Bridge. Freshwater influence from Rocky Creek makes flounder fishing excellent, and the glass-calm state park coves are ideal for sight-fishing redfish. Mangrove snapper hold near dock pilings, adding a target species not commonly found at other dashboard locations. It is the furthest location from Shipyard Marina, so factor run time into your planning.
rocky.todaysCall: Rocky Bayou's state park setting and calm coves make it a genuine refuge on a stormy day — once you're inside, ESE winds at 15 mph are largely a non-issue and you'll have protected water even as conditions build elsewhere. The slightly stained water will limit sight-fishing for reds in the coves somewhat, so complement that approach by targeting dock pilings for mangrove snapper and working flounder-friendly structure near the Rocky Creek influence. The morning falling tide toward the 11:14 AM low is your most productive window — flounder and reds will be feeding actively as water moves. The distance from Shipyard Marina is the biggest variable today; leave early and plan your turnaround time carefully given the 83% storm chance after 11 AM. If you're already close to Niceville or launching locally, this is an excellent low-pressure, high-quality option for a summer storm day.
